critic_raw: The e-commerce transformer ranker design is detailed. I want to probe the review text inclusion. The design includes 'concatenated recent review text (2000 tokens, sorted by recency)' for the transformer. Review text is included 'raw, unfiltered, and unfiltered to allow the transformer to learn which aspects of user feedback correlate with relevance.' This 2000 tokens of review text combined with 512 tokens of query and title gives a potential sequence of ~2500 tokens total — but BERT-style models have a maximum 512-token context. How are 2000 tokens of review text handled with a 512-token limit?
defender_raw: The design mentions a BERT-style cross-encoder architecture. The stated 'max 512 tokens' applies to the query and title tokens, while the 2000 review tokens are processed separately. The design says '[CLS, query tokens, [SEP], title tokens, [SEP], review tokens, numerical features]' — this is concatenated and presumably handled by a model with a longer context window (e.g., Longformer or a BERT with extended context) or through hierarchical processing. The '512 tokens' in the feature description likely refers to query and title components individually, not the total. If a standard BERT (512 max tokens) is used, truncation of review text would be necessary. This is an implementation detail that should be specified clearly in the design. The comparison would still be valid if review text is truncated for the transformer — the hypothesis is whether review text adds value.
adjudication_raw: One concern: 2000 review tokens + query/title tokens may exceed BERT's 512-token limit — architectural inconsistency in the design. Defender correctly notes this is an implementation detail; a longer-context model or truncation would handle it; the comparison remains valid either way. No fundamental methodological flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
